name: Python Sets
description: Questions on sets: definition, access, add, remove, loop, join, frozenset, and set methods.

questions.question: Which operation is NOT O(1) average for a Python set?
questions.explanation: Sorted iteration requires O(n log n) because sets are unordered. You must sort first.

questions.question: What does {1, 2, 2, 3} evaluate to?
questions.explanation: Sets contain unique elements; duplicates are automatically removed.

questions.question: Which set operation returns elements in either set?
questions.explanation: Union (| or union()) returns all elements from both sets.

questions.question: Can a set contain a tuple?
choices: Yes, if the tuple contains only hashable items
questions.explanation: Sets require hashable elements. Tuples are hashable if all their elements are hashable.

questions.question: What is the result of set()?
questions.explanation: set() is the empty set. {} is an empty dict, not a set.

questions.question: What best describes a Python set?
choices: Unordered collection of unique, hashable elements
Same as a list but faster
questions.explanation: A set is an unordered collection of unique elements. All elements must be hashable (immutable in practice).

questions.question: Why can't a set contain a list?
questions.explanation: Set elements must be hashable. Lists are mutable and therefore unhashable, so they cannot be set elements.

questions.question: Can you do s[0] to get the first element of a set s?
questions.explanation: Sets are unordered and not subscriptable. Use s.pop() to get an arbitrary element, or iterate with for x in s.

questions.question: How do you check if x is in a set s?
questions.explanation: Use the membership operator: x in s. It is O(1) average for sets. Use s.__contains__(x) only in special cases.

questions.question: What does s.add(x) do?
choices: Adds x if not already in the set
Replaces the first element with x
Adds x and returns the set
questions.explanation: add(x) adds x to the set if it is not already present. It returns None and modifies the set in place.

questions.question: What does s.update([1, 2, 3]) do?
choices: Adds the list as a single element
Adds 1, 2, and 3 as elements to the set
questions.explanation: update(iterable) adds each element from the iterable to the set. So update([1,2,3]) adds 1, 2, and 3.

questions.question: What happens if you call s.remove(x) when x is not in the set?
questions.explanation: remove(x) raises KeyError if x is not in the set. Use discard(x) to remove x if present without raising an error.

questions.question: What happens if you call s.discard(x) when x is not in the set?
questions.explanation: discard(x) removes x if it is in the set; if not, it does nothing and does not raise an error.

questions.question: What does s.pop() do?
choices: Removes and returns an arbitrary element
Returns the set without changing it
questions.explanation: pop() removes and returns an arbitrary element. Raises KeyError if the set is empty. Order is not defined.

questions.question: What does s.clear() do?
choices: Removes all elements from the set
questions.explanation: clear() removes every element from the set, leaving it empty. It returns None and modifies the set in place.

questions.question: Is the iteration order of a set guaranteed?
choices: No; order is arbitrary and may vary
questions.explanation: Sets are unordered. Iteration order is not guaranteed and can change between runs or versions. Use sorted(s) for a fixed order.

questions.question: " ".join(s) when s is a set of strings: does it work?
choices: Yes; join accepts any iterable, but order is arbitrary
questions.explanation: str.join() accepts any iterable of strings. A set works, but the order of concatenation is arbitrary (not insertion or alphabetical).

questions.question: What is a frozenset?
choices: A set that can only hold numbers
questions.explanation: frozenset is an immutable set. It has no add, remove, or update methods and is hashable, so it can be a dict key or set element.

questions.question: Can a frozenset be used as a dict key?
questions.explanation: frozenset is hashable and immutable, so it can be a dict key or an element of another set. Plain set cannot.

questions.question: Which set method returns elements that are in both sets?
questions.explanation: intersection (& or intersection()) returns elements that are in both sets. a & b or a.intersection(b).

questions.question: Which set method returns elements in the first set but not the second?
questions.explanation: difference (- or difference()) returns elements in the first set that are not in the second. a - b or a.difference(b).

questions.question: What does symmetric_difference return?
choices: Elements in either set but not in both
Elements only in the first set
questions.explanation: symmetric_difference (^ or symmetric_difference()) returns elements that are in either set but not in both.

questions.question: s.issubset(t) is True when?
choices: Every element of s is in t
Every element of t is in s
s and t have no elements in common
questions.explanation: issubset(t) (or s <= t) is True if every element of s is in t. So s is a subset of t.

questions.question: s.isdisjoint(t) is True when?
choices: s is a subset of t
s and t have no elements in common
questions.explanation: isdisjoint(t) returns True if s and t have no elements in common. Useful to check for no overlap before combining.

questions.question: What is the difference between difference() and difference_update()?
choices: difference() returns a new set; difference_update() modifies in place
difference() modifies in place; difference_update() returns a new set
questions.explanation: difference(others) returns a new set (s unchanged). difference_update(others) removes from s the elements that are in the others and returns None.
